mainmatter.p: Mike Shulman has sent me a potential proof of quasi-idempotence for the generic element of a semicoherent cone. This relies on a “fattened” definition of semicoherence in the wild-categorical setting, similar to Mike’s fattened version in the special case of subuniverses. Mike points out that the condition, when specialized for cones over the identity functor, is much simpler than the general case. I am thinking that I might see if it is possible to formalize it in TypeTopology.

Mike Shulman has sent me a potential proof of quasi-idempotence for the generic element of a semicoherent cone. This relies on a “fattened” definition of semicoherence in the wild-categorical setting, similar to Mike’s fattened version in the special case of subuniverses. Mike points out that the condition, when specialized for cones over the identity functor, is much simpler than the general case. I am thinking that I might see if it is possible to formalize it in TypeTopology.

Mike Shulman has sent me a potential proof of quasi-idempotence for the generic element of a semicoherent cone. This relies on a “fattened” definition of semicoherence in the wild-categorical setting, similar to Mike’s fattened version in the special case of subuniverses. Mike points out that the condition, when specialized for cones over the identity functor, is much simpler than the general case. I am thinking that I might see if it is possible to formalize it in TypeTopology.

Let \mathcal{C} be a wild category; a quasi-idempotent on an object A: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} } is defined to be a pre-idempotent { \left ( f, \phi \right )} on A together with an identification \chi between f; \phi : f; { \left ( f;f \right )} = f;f and the following composite identification:
mainmatter.tex: f; { \left ( f;f \right )} \xrightarrow{\mathsf{assoc} _{ \mathcal{C} } \, f \, f \, f} { \left ( f;f \right )} ;f \xrightarrow{\phi ;f} f;f

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V} be an impredicative universe pair with A: \mathcal{V} such that \mathsf{INatStr} is the displayed reflection algebra of incoherent naturality structures over the product { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} X of all reflection algebras. We define a further displayed reflection algebra \mathsf{SemiCoh} over \mathsf{INatStr} of “semicoherence structures” associating to each incoherent naturality structure one dimension of coherence data for binary compositions of homomorphisms.
In particular, we define \mathsf{SemiCoh} over \widetilde{\mathsf{INatStr}} as follows, employing our notion of homotopy between homomorphisms:

To define the displayed algebra structure \bar \eta _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} }, we will use the contractibility of singletons several times. In particular, given { \left ( Y,f \right )} where Y: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} and f:X \multimap Y, the algebra component \eta _{ Y } is completely determined by the homotopy \eta _{ f } : { \eta _{ Y } } \sim _{ A \to Y } { f \circ \eta _{ X } }; likewise, by our characterization of paths between homomorphisms, the pair { \left ( h, \phi \right )} is uniquely determined. Therefore, we may define \bar \eta _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} } to be the unique function satisfying the following equation:

Given an element u: \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}, we shall write \epsilon _{ u } , \vartheta _{ u } for the components of its underlying incoherent naturality structure \pi _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} } u and \varsigma _{ u } : \mathsf{SemiCoh} [ \pi _{ \mathsf{SemiCoh} } u] for the semicoherence structure itself.
This definition is similar to that of Awodey, Frey, and Speight, except that we do not impose any coherence law for identities.

Let \mathcal{C} , \mathcal{D} be two wild categories, and let F,G: \mathcal{C} \to \mathcal{D} be two wild 1-semi-functors from \mathcal{C} to \mathcal{D}. A wild semi-coherent natural transformation is defined by the data of a wild natural transformation \alpha : F \to G together with, for each f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )} and g : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( B , C \right )}, a higher identification \mathsf{seq} _{ \alpha } \, f \, g between \alpha _{ f;g } : F { \left ( f;g \right )} ; \alpha _{ C } = \alpha _{ A } ;G { \left ( f;g \right )} and the composite identification:

frontmatter.title: TypeTopology

mainmatter.p: Various new theorems in univalent mathematics written in Agda. By Martín Hötzel Escardó and collaborators.
